title: Why You Feel Exhausted After Doing Nothing
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Discover why complex trauma leads to profound exhaustion, even when you've done nothing. Learn how to heal the nervous system's chronic freeze response.
imageDescription: A single, delicate dandelion seed floats in the warm, golden light of a sunrise. The background is a soft, out-of-focus meadow, evoking a sense of peace, release, and gentle forward movement.
htmlContent:
What happens when the body’s alarm system gets stuck in the “on” position, long after the threat has passed? What happens when the emergency brake is pulled, but the car is already safely in the garage? a genuine sense of depletion that has nothing to do with the day’s activities and everything to do with a nervous system perpetually braced for impact.

The Ghost in the Machine: Understanding Functional Freeze

We often think of the trauma response in terms of fight or flight... a dramatic surge of energy to either confront or escape a threat. But there is a third, more insidious response: the freeze. This isn’t just about being paralyzed with fear in a single moment; for those with C-PTSD, it can become a chronic, low-grade state of being. What we find here is the functional freeze, a state where one can go through the motions of daily life ~ work, errands, conversations... while the underlying physiology is in a state of shutdown. It’s a survival mechanism that was once significantly intelligent, a way to endure the unendurable by numbing out and conserving energy. The body learns that the world is not a safe place, and so it remains in a state of high alert, even in the absence of any discernible danger. This constant, low-level activation of the sympathetic nervous system, combined with the dorsal vagal shutdown of the freeze response, is an immense drain on our metabolic resources. It’s like running a marathon in your sleep, every single night. You wake up tired because your body has been working so hard to keep you safe from threats that exist only as echoes in your nervous system. This is not a failure of the mind, but a triumph of the body's innate wisdom, a survival strategy that has outlived its usefulness. The exhaustion is a symptom, not the disease itself. It is a messenger, asking us to pay attention to the parts of ourselves that have been holding on for so long.

The Energetic Cost of Hypervigilance

Hypervigilance is another hallmark of complex trauma, and it is one of the primary culprits behind this pervasive exhaustion. It is the practice of constantly scanning the environment for signs of danger, both real and imagined. Every flicker of movement, every shift in tone of voice, every unexpected sound is processed as a potential threat. It turns out to be not a conscious choice, but rather a deeply ingrained pattern of the amygdala, the brain’s smoke detector. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve sat with people who describe this experience as being a detective in their own lives, constantly searching for clues to a crime that has already happened. This ceaseless scanning, this perpetual state of readiness, is incredibly taxing. It’s no wonder that by the end of the day, or even by midday, there is simply nothing left in the tank. The energy has been spent not on living, but on surviving. This is a real misallocation of life force, a diversion of resources from growth and connection to mere existence. The tragedy is not just the energy that is lost, but the life that is not lived, the joy that is not felt, the connection that is not made. The exhaustion is the receipt for a debt that was never yours to pay.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

The Body as a Living Archive

The Myth of Relaxation

The Compass of Sensation

The way out of this labyrinth of exhaustion is not through the mind, but through the body. The body has a language, a grammar of sensation that is far older and more reliable than the language of thought. The work is to learn to listen to this language, to become fluent in the subtle dialect of our own physiology. That becomes the practice of interoception, of turning the attention inward and learning to track the ever-shifting field of sensation. It is about noticing the tightness in the chest, the hollowness in the stomach, the heat in the face, not as problems to be solved, but as communications to be received. These sensations are not random; they are the body’s way of telling its story. By bringing a gentle, non-judgmental awareness to these sensations, we begin to thaw the frozen parts of ourselves, to bring warmth and circulation to places that have been cold and numb for years. This is not about catharsis or dramatic emotional release; it is a quiet, steady process of titration, of introducing small amounts of sensation and allowing the nervous system to integrate them at its own pace. It is a dance of pendulation, of moving between the discomfort of the trauma vortex and the safety of the present moment. This is how we build resilience, not by avoiding the pain, but by learning to be with it in a way that does not overwhelm the system. We learn more about this in our article on Titration and Pendulation in Trauma Healing.

The Tyranny of the Urgent

In a world that glorifies productivity and constant motion, the experience of trauma-related exhaustion can be a source of significant shame and self-judgment. We live in a culture that pathologizes rest and equates stillness with laziness. This creates a painful internal conflict for those whose bodies are crying out for a pause. The pressure to “keep up” and “push through” can lead to a cycle of boom and bust, of overextending oneself and then crashing into a state of collapse. The work here is to begin to challenge this internalized capitalism, to recognize that your worth is not determined by your output. It is to cultivate a radical self-compassion that honors the body’s need for rest, not as a reward for productivity, but as a fundamental human right. This is a pioneering act in a world that is constantly demanding more. It is a declaration of sovereignty over your own nervous system, a reclaiming of your right to exist outside of the narrow confines of what our culture deems “productive.”

The Architecture of Returning

So how do we begin to address this striking exhaustion? The answer does not lie in trying to force the body to relax, but in gently and incrementally teaching it that it is safe. What emerges is the work of somatic inquiry, of learning to listen to the body’s subtle cues and respond to them with compassion and curiosity. It is about creating small moments of safety, what I call “islands of okayness,” in the vast ocean of hypervigilance. These are not attempts to escape the discomfort, but rather to anchor yourself in the present moment, to provide the nervous system with new data that contradicts the old story of threat. A client once described this as building a new house, one brick at a time, while the old one is still standing. We don’t have to demolish the old structure all at once; we simply have to start building a new one, a place where we can eventually come to rest. For those interested in the practical application of these concepts, our article on polyvagal exercises for anxiety offers some gentle starting points.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

The Slow Unwinding

Healing from complex trauma is not a linear process. It is a slow, often messy, and deeply personal road of unwinding. There will be days when the exhaustion feels all-consuming, when it feels like you are taking two steps back for every one step forward. It turns out to be not a sign of failure, but a natural part of the process. The body is not a machine that can be fixed overnight. It is a living organism with its own intelligence and its own timeline. This requires a radical kind of patience, a willingness to be with the discomfort without trying to fix it or push it away. It is about learning to trust the body’s innate capacity to heal, even when the mind is filled with doubt and despair. This is a path of a thousand small steps, each one proof of your courage and your resilience. If you’re struggling with the feeling of being stuck, you may find our article on what to do when you feel stuck to be a compassionate guide. This road is not about arriving at a destination called "healed." It is about learning to inhabit the process itself, to find the beauty and the wisdom in the unfolding. It is about making a home in the wilderness of your own being, and discovering that you are far more resilient than you ever imagined.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel more tired on my days off?

What we find here is a common experience for those with a history of complex trauma. When you are busy and distracted, your nervous system is in a state of high activation, which can mask the underlying exhaustion. It’s a sign that your body is finally getting the chance to do the work of healing.

Is it possible to ever not feel this tired?

Yes, it is absolutely possible. Healing from complex trauma is a long work, but it is a process that leads to a place of greater ease and vitality. It is a slow process of reclaiming your life force, one small step at a time.

For brain health and mood support, a well-regarded option is Nordic Naturals Ultimate Omega (paid link).

Let that land for a moment.

What is one small thing I can do to start?

Begin by bringing a gentle, curious attention to the sensations in your body. You don’t need to change anything or fix anything. Simply notice. Notice the temperature of the air on your skin. Feel the gentle movement of your breath. These small moments of embodied awareness are the building blocks of a regulated nervous system and a more resilient life.

An adaptogen that some people find helpful for stress is Jarrow Formulas Ashwagandha (paid link).

The process out of the freeze is not a race. It is a slow and tender return to the wisdom of the body. It is about trusting that, even in the depths of the exhaustion, there is a current of life that is always, always flowing towards wholeness.
